name: Cova de Camp Duai
coordinates_utm: Contact the Balearic Speleology Federation (DATUM ETRS89)
equipment: 1 rope of 25 m for the entrance, 1 rope of 20 m for the shaft between upper and intermediate levels, 1 sling of at least 2 m for a small jump past Mud Alley, 1 protector, 2 bolts, carabiners
description: A multi-level cave in Sant Llorenç reaching 78 m deep, historically used for tobacco smuggling. The upper level features alveolar chambers with formations and roots, while the CO2 Chamber contains an impressive corrosion pit. The intermediate level is the most spectacular, comparable only to Cova des Pas de Vallgornera, with pristine white speleothems including thousands of helictites, tree-like stalagmites covered in botryoidal formations, and translucent efflorescent crystals.
full_description:
Description of the cavity

After overcoming a 22 m descent, the last 15 m in free air, we 'land' in this alveolar-type hall, not lacking in formations nor in the multitude of roots that cover part of its walls and ceiling. In appearance, a classic hall like so many others on the island, which however shows no signs of what this cave conceals. The surface is covered with clasts fallen from the exterior, as well as bones from different mammal species. Near the rope there is a dry-stone construction (a type of bench), which was part of a pulley system used to lower contraband tobacco that was stored in the cave.

CO2 HALL

This hall, in addition to beautiful formations, houses the Carbide Well (Pou des Carburo), with dimensions of approximately one meter in diameter and a depth of ten, presenting significant levels of corrosion clearly visible on its walls, as well as on the calcium carbonate flowstone above the mouth, reaching a height exceeding two and a half meters. Not far away, just a few meters, there is another interesting vent, although due to its inaccessibility its dimensions are unknown. Outside the cave, just 25 m from the entrance at a bearing of 25° N, there is a small well currently filled with stones inside, and it is unknown whether it was ever directly connected to the cave.

THE NEW WELLS (ES NOUS POUS)

This is a structural hall of considerable dimensions, in which a gigantic subsidence process collapsed it, revealing a beautiful tepee that runs longitudinally from one end to the other. This event also created a beautiful fault mirror in the NE zone. A crack of significant size, as well as some chimneys in the central part of the hall, absorb the rainwater from the exterior, charged with a significant concentration of CO₂ capable of corroding the calcite flowstone over which the water flows. Formations are not lacking in this hall; in fact, they abound copiously both in quantity and variety everywhere, with great activity appreciable in the hall, especially in the central zone.

THE SUFFERING (ES PATIMENT)

After crossing the Mud Alley (Carreró des Fang) and descending a small jump, one reaches a beautiful ceiling corrosion cupola, which precedes a kind of window that opens onto one of the most beautiful areas of the cave. The abundance and variety of speleothems in this place is immense; the helictites can be counted in the thousands, of all sizes, thicknesses, and shapes, pointing in all directions, even developing vertically from the floor. Notable in this hall is a most beautiful column largely covered in eccentric formations and acicular crystals, some of which have dagger-like morphology.

INTERMEDIATE LEVEL

Once the level has been changed (letter 'F' on the survey), in progressive descent, we approach the part that makes this cavity unique. This is the level with the greatest extent, measuring 36 x 75 m. Only the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera can compete with everything that has developed here over time. Pure extreme whiteness in the forms generated by the electromagnetic energy that orders the axial sliding of the crystals, translucent efflorescences over the lumpy carbonate coating. In the depths and various recesses of the cavern — a sort of enormous geode — located between the -35 meter mark and the seventy-something meters, as far as the speleologist can transit in depth, across the length, width, and height of the steep, narrow or scarped slope. The speleothems in this place are impressive, not only for their immaculate whiteness but also, on occasion, for their originality — such is the case of the singular stalagmite of arboreal structure, large in size and completely covered in botryoidal formations.
